items.mistake: Tuning hyperparameters using training error only
items.fix: Always select complexity, regularization, and stopping point using validation or cross-validated error.
items.why: Training error always improves with more capacity — it cannot reveal overfitting.
items.example: Polynomial degree 15 minimizes train MSE but val MSE spikes; degree 3 wins on validation.

items.mistake: Adding more data when both train and validation error are high
items.fix: Increase model capacity, add features, or reduce excessive regularization first; re-check learning curves.
items.why: Underfitting is often high bias — extra rows shrink variance but not structural misspecification.
items.example: Linear model on quadratic data: 10k vs 100k rows leave similar train MSE; degree-2 features fix both errors.

items.mistake: Assuming low training error means the model is production-ready
items.fix: Compare validation/test error and monitor the train–val gap before release.
items.why: Near-zero train error with poor validation is classic overfitting — poor generalization.
items.example: Depth-20 tree: 100% train accuracy, 62% validation — reject for deployment.

items.mistake: Applying heavy regularization when the model already underfits
items.fix: Confirm training error is reasonably low before increasing L2, dropout, or pruning.
items.why: Regularization increases bias — harmful when capacity is already the bottleneck.
items.example: 50% dropout on a tiny net with 45% train F1 — loosen dropout and widen the network first.
